1: wayyĕhî kî-hēḥēl hāʾādām lārōb ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmāh: "When human beings began to increase in number on the face of the ground." Hēḥēl (from ḥālal, H2490, to begin) — the same verb used in 4:26 for the beginning of worship. Here it is the beginning of multiplication — the fulfillment of the creation mandate of Genesis 1:28 ("be fruitful and multiply") that now, within a corrupted humanity, produces the conditions for judgment. Multiplication was the blessing; in a fallen world it also multiplies wickedness.
2: bĕnê hāʾĕlōhîm: "Sons of God." This phrase occurs in Job 1:6; Genesis 2:1; 38:7 as a term for angelic or divine beings in the heavenly court. Three principal interpretations have been defended: (1) Divine beings (angels/Watchers) — the reading of 1 Enoch, the LXX, and many Church Fathers; supported by the Job parallels; (2) Sethites — the line of Seth (sons of God by covenant), marrying into the Cainite line (daughters of men) — supported by Augustine, Calvin, and others; (3) Dynasts/kings — ancient Near Eastern royal ideology in which kings are called "sons of god"; supported by some ANE comparative evidence. The MKT's "sons of God" preserves the Hebrew without resolving the dispute. Yāpôt (good/beautiful) — the same term used of the six days of creation ("God saw that it was good"); the corruption ironically uses the creator's evaluative vocabulary.
3: lōʾ-yādôn rûḥî bāʾādām lĕʿōlām: "My Spirit will not contend with humans forever." Yādôn is one of the most debated words in the OT; it occurs only here and its root is uncertain. Major options: (1) from dûn (to judge/contend) — "my Spirit will not judge/strive with humanity forever"; (2) from a root meaning "to remain/abide" — "my Spirit will not remain in humanity forever." The LXX reads "remain" (kataménō). The MKT's "contend" follows the majority tradition. Bĕšaggam hûʾ bāśār: "because/for they are flesh" — bāśār (H1320, flesh) here denotes mortal weakness and creatureliness, not sexuality. "120 years" is ambiguous: a deadline for the flood or a new maximum human lifespan. The post-flood genealogy's decreasing lifespans eventually reach 120 (Moses, Deuteronomy 34:7), supporting the latter reading.
4: hannĕpīlîm: "The Nephilim." The word nĕpîlîm (H5303) appears only here and in Numbers 13:33. The root may be nāpal (to fall) — "fallen ones" — or it may be a foreign loanword. The LXX translates as gigantes (giants). The text calls them haggibborîm ʾanšê haššēm — "the mighty men, men of renown" (šēm = name). The phrase ʾanšê haššēm ("men of the name") stands in deliberate contrast to the call to "call upon the name (šēm) of YHWH" in Genesis 4:26 — these are men who have made themselves famous rather than calling on God's name. The Nephilim appear "also afterward" — Numbers 13:33 places them in Canaan; the text leaves their post-flood presence unexplained.
5: kol-yēṣer maḥšĕbōt libbô raq raʿ kol-hayyôm: "Every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day." Yēṣer (H3336, inclination/impulse, from yāṣar, to form/fashion) — the same root as the potter-forming of Genesis 2:7. The formed shape of human mental life, its yēṣer, is characterized as raq raʿ kol-hayyôm: only (raq) evil (raʿ) all the day (kol-hayyôm). The three-term intensification allows no exception: the totality of human cognitive inclination, at every moment, tends toward evil. This verse becomes the foundation of the rabbinic concept of the yēṣer hāraʿ (evil inclination) and the Reformed doctrine of total depravity — not that humans do nothing good outwardly, but that the governing orientation of the fallen heart is corrupted at the root.
6: wayyinnāḥem YHWH kî-ʿāśāh ʾet-hāʾādām bāʾāreṣ wayyitʿaṣṣēb ʾel-libbô: "YHWH regretted that he had made humanity on the earth, and his heart was deeply troubled." Nāḥam (H5162, to sigh/regret/comfort) — the same root as Noah's name. Here it denotes genuine divine emotional response to a changed situation, what theologians call anthropopathism (attributing human-like emotions to God for communicative purposes). The verb is not retraction of omniscience; the OT elsewhere affirms God does not repent as humans do (1 Samuel 15:29; Numbers 23:19). The "regret" is the grief of a covenant-making God when his creatures betray the covenant — genuine relational pain, not ignorance about outcomes. Wayyitʿaṣṣēb ʾel-libbô (his heart was pained) — ʿāṣab (H6087) is the same root used of the pain in childbearing (Genesis 3:16) and the painful labor of the ground (Genesis 3:17); God's grief over humanity's sin uses the vocabulary of the curse that human sin introduced.
7: ʾemḥeh ʾet-hāʾādām ʾăšer-bārāʾtî: "I will wipe out the humanity that I created." Māḥāh (H4229, to blot out/erase/wipe away) is a strong verb — used of blotting out names (Deuteronomy 9:14), blotting out sin (Isaiah 43:25), and the erasure of Amalek (Deuteronomy 25:19). The word choice is extreme: not exile or punishment but annihilation of what was created. The divine speech includes animals, birds, and creatures (šereṣ, crawling things) — the non-human creation suffers because it was made for the sake of humanity and is caught in the consequences of human sin (cf. Romans 8:20-22).
8: wĕnōaḥ māṣāʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê YHWH: "But Noah found favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of YHWH." This is the first occurrence of ḥēn (H2580, grace/favor) in the Bible — 69 occurrences follow in the OT. The word denotes the free, unmerited goodwill of a superior toward an inferior; it is not earned, which is why it is "found" (māṣāʾ) rather than achieved. The NT equivalent charis (grace) derives conceptually from this word. The structural placement of this verse is decisive: v7 ends with God's decision to annihilate; v8 begins with "but Noah" — the exception is pure gift.
9: nōaḥ ʾîš ṣaddîq tāmîm hāyāh bĕdōrōtāyw ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm hithallēk-nōaḥ: "Noah was a righteous man, blameless among the people of his time; Noah walked with God." Three terms characterize Noah: (1) ṣaddîq (H6662, righteous/just) — the covenant-legal term for one whose conduct conforms to the covenant standard; (2) tāmîm (H8535, blameless/wholesome/complete) — used of sacrificial animals without defect (Leviticus 1:3) and of Job (Job 1:1); together the two words form the OT gold standard for human righteousness; (3) hithallēk ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm — the same Hithpael formula as Enoch (Genesis 5:22,24), denoting continuous walking with God. Hebrews 11:7 adds the crucial qualifier: "by faith" — Noah's righteousness was covenantally constituted, not self-achieved.
10: wayyôled nōaḥ šĕlōšāh bānîm ʾet-šēm ʾet-ḥām wĕʾet-yāpet: "And Noah fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth." The ordering recurs throughout Genesis 6-10. Šēm (name/renown) is listed first and will carry the Semitic/covenant lineage. Ḥām (Ham) may mean "hot" or be connected to the Egyptian term for Egypt (Kmt); Yāpet (Japheth) may derive from pāṯāh (to open/enlarge) — cf. Genesis 9:27 ("God shall enlarge Japheth"). The three sons are the post-flood prototype of human diversity: one family, three trajectories.
11: wattišāḥēt hāʾāreṣ lipnê hāʾĕlōhîm wattimālēʾ hāʾāreṣ ḥāmās: "The earth was corrupt before God and the earth was full of violence." Šāḥat (H7843, to corrupt/destroy/ruin) appears three times in vv11-12 (corrupt, corrupt, corrupted) — the triple occurrence emphasizes totality. Ḥāmās (H2555, violence) encompasses physical violence, social injustice, and covenant violation; it is the term used of the "witness of violence" in Exodus 23:1 and appears throughout the prophets as a charge against corrupt societies (Isaiah 53:9; Jeremiah 6:7; Micah 6:12; Habakkuk 1:2-3). The combination of šāḥat and ḥāmās defines the pre-flood condition: internal corruption producing outward violence.
12: wayyarʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hāʾāreṣ wĕhinnēh nišḥātāh kî-hišḥît kol-bāśār ʾet-darkô ʿal-hāʾāreṣ: "God saw the earth, and behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted their way on the earth." The divine seeing (rāʾāh) echoes the repeated "God saw that it was good" of Genesis 1; here God sees what humanity has made of the good creation. Kol-bāśār (all flesh) — this phrase (used 9 times in the flood narrative) becomes a technical designation for the totality of embodied creaturely life under human dominion. All flesh has hišḥît ʾet-darkô — corrupted its way — humanity has destroyed what it was meant to tend.
13: qēṣ kol-bāśār bāʾ lĕpānay: "The end of all flesh has come before me." Qēṣ (H7093, end/extremity) is the word used in Amos 8:2 ("The end has come upon my people Israel") and Ezekiel 7:2 ("The end! The end has come"). The flood is announced with the same eschatological vocabulary the prophets will later use for divine judgment on Israel. ʾĂnî mašḥîtām ʾet-hāʾāreṣ: "I am going to destroy them with the earth" — God uses the same root (šāḥat) that described humanity's self-corruption: the destroyer of the earth will be destroyed by the same word that named their offense.
14: ʿăśēh lĕkā tēbat ʿăṣê-gōper: "Make yourself an ark of gopher-wood." Tēbāh (H8392, ark) occurs elsewhere in the OT only in Exodus 2:3,5 (Moses's basket) — same word, different scale. Both are rescue-vessels that preserve covenant-bearers through waters of death into a new era. ʿăṣê-gōper (gopher-wood) — the only occurrence of gōper in the OT; the species is unknown, though some equate it with cypress or cedar. Wĕkāpartāh ʾōtāh mibbayt ûmiḥûṣ bakkōper: "coat it inside and out with pitch." Kōper (H3724, pitch/bitumen) shares the consonantal root with kāpar (to cover/atone, H3722) — the same root as kippûr (atonement, Day of Atonement). The bituminous sealing that protects the ark from the waters of judgment uses the same linguistic root as the atoning cover that protects humanity from the wrath of God — a philological connection recognized in rabbinic midrash and many commentators across traditions.
15: šĕlōš mēʾôt ʾammāh ōrek hattēbāh ḥămiššîm ʾammāh rāḥbāh ûšĕlōšîm ʾammāh qōmātāh: "Three hundred cubits long, fifty wide, and thirty high." The cubit (ʾammāh, H520) is approximately 18 inches (45 cm) by the standard cubit, making the ark roughly 450 x 75 x 45 feet (137 x 23 x 14 meters). The proportions (length:width:height = Genesis 300:50:30 = Genesis 6:1:0.6) match modern naval architecture's stability ratios for seagoing cargo vessels. Ancient Near Eastern parallels (the Gilgamesh Epic's ark is cubical) differ markedly in design; the Genesis ark's elongated proportions are more practically seaworthy.
16: ṣōhar taʿăśeh lattēbāh wĕʾel-ʾammāh tĕkallennāh milmāʿlāh: "Make a roof for it, and finish it to a cubit above." Ṣōhar (H6672) appears only here and its meaning is uncertain: "light-opening," "roof," or "midday light" — the architectural detail may denote a ventilation band around the top of the ark. Petaḥ hattēbāh bĕṣiddāh tāśîm: "Put the door of the ark in its side." A single door and three decks define the only entrance into safety — a structural detail that typologists note alongside John 10:9 ("I am the door") and Genesis 14:6 ("I am the way").
17: waʾănî hinnĕnî mēbîʾ ʾet-hammabbûl mayim: "And I, behold, I am bringing the floodwaters." Mabbûl (H3999, flood/deluge) appears 13 times, all in the flood narrative except Psalms 29:10 ("YHWH sat enthroned over the flood"); it is a technical term for the primordial waters. WaʾĂnî hinnĕnî (and I, behold) — the divine self-emphasis marks a solemn declaration of intent. Kol-ʾăšer-bô rûaḥ ḥayyîm: "everything in which is the breath of life" — rûaḥ ḥayyîm (breath/spirit of life) echoes Genesis 2:7 (God breathed the nišmat ḥayyîm); what God breathed into Adam will be extinguished in all who receive no protection.
18: wahăqimōtî ʾet-bĕrîtî ʾittāk: "But I will establish my covenant with you." The first use of bĕrît (H1285, covenant) in the Bible occurs here, before the formal covenantal elaboration after the flood in Genesis 9. Hēqîm bĕrît (to establish/confirm a covenant) is the technical Hiphil formula for activating a previously existing or newly initiated covenant; it implies the covenant has a prior basis (in God's purposes) that is now being formally actualized with Noah. The Noahic covenant is not the first divine-human relationship but the first formally denominated bĕrît — the structural form that will govern all subsequent biblical theology of election and salvation.
19: ûmikol-haḥay mikkol-bāśār šĕnayim mikkōl tābîʾ ʾel-hattēbāh: "And of every living thing, of all flesh, two of every kind you shall bring into the ark." The command uses lĕhaḥăyōt ʾittāk (to keep alive with you) — the purpose of the ark is preservation of life, not just human survival. The taxonomic pairs signal a restoration of the original creative order (Gen 1 establishes the species; the ark preserves the species through judgment). Zākār ûnĕqēbāh (male and female) — the creation binary of 1:27 becomes the survival binary; the reproductive pair is the minimum viable unit for continuation.
20: mēhāʿôp lĕmînēhû ûmin-habbĕhēmāh lĕmînāh: "Of the birds according to their kinds, and of the animals according to their kinds." The lĕmîn (according to kind/species) formula repeats seven times in Gen 1 as the marker of created order; here it recurs as the organizing principle for what will be preserved. The ark is not saving random survivors; it is preserving the created order — the kinds that God made in the beginning — through the judgment that fallen humanity has brought on the creation.
21: wĕʾattāh qaḥ-lĕkā mikkol-maʾăkāl ʾăšer yēʾākēl: "And you, take with you every kind of food that is eaten." The provision of food for the preservation period is a practical divine instruction that underscores the ark's nature: it is not a magical transport but a functional vessel requiring actual preparation and sustaining resources. God commands what Noah must actively do; Noah will do it (v22). The pairing of divine instruction and human obedient action is the pattern of covenant faithfulness throughout Scripture.
22: wayyaʿas nōaḥ kĕkōl ʾăšer ṣiwwāh ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm kēn ʿāśāh: "Noah did according to all that God commanded him; so he did." The verse is emphatic: kĕkōl ... kēn ʿāśāh (according to all ... so he did). The complete compliance formula echoes Gen 1's "and it was so" — the language of creation is reused for the covenant instruction. What was achieved in creation by divine fiat is achieved in covenant by human obedient response to the divine word. Hebrews 11:7 identifies this as faith: "By faith Noah... in holy fear built an ark."
